title: most people building ai tutoring think the hard part is the
description: AI tutoring earns family trust through auditable progress evidence and real safety controls before the tutoring experience itself.
tldr: The model is not the hard part of AI tutoring. Families need on-demand evidence of what a child did and clear proof of what the tool refused to do.

sections.h2: why is a smart chatbot not enough for AI tutoring?
body: A parent is deciding whether to place their child’s afternoons inside your product. A chatbot that sounds smart does not answer the questions behind that decision.
They need to see what the child actually did and understand the boundaries the tool held.

sections.h2: what does a family need before an AI tutor becomes part of the routine?
body: Auditable progress evidence and real safety controls are the price of entry into a family’s routine.
Those requirements make the experience legible on demand. They give a parent something concrete to inspect when deciding whether the product deserves trust.

sections.h2: what should an AI tutoring builder build first?
body: The receipt shows what happened during a child’s time with the tool and what the tool refused to do. Once that exists, the tutoring is the easy half.

faqs.q: What makes an AI tutoring product trustworthy to parents?
faqs.a: Parents need on-demand visibility into what their child actually did. They also need real safety controls and evidence of the boundaries the tool enforced.

faqs.q: Is the model the hardest part of building AI tutoring?
faqs.a: The model is not the hard part described here. The harder requirement is earning a place in a family’s routine through evidence and safety controls.

faqs.q: What does “build the receipt first” mean for AI tutoring?
faqs.a: It means building the evidence a parent can inspect on demand. That evidence covers the child’s activity and what the tool refused to do.
